title: Multisource Multimodal Calibration for Precise Disconnector Recognition in Power Grids
authors: Yucen Fan, Jingsong Hu
abstract: Accurate extrinsic calibration between light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and cameras is essential for multisensor fusion in industrial perception. Most existing approaches rely on rigid sensor rigs and synchronized capture, which limits their applicability to real-world industrial scenarios where sensors are often deployed independently or operate asynchronously. In contrast, this article presents a structure-aware, targetless calibration framework that enables reliable LiDAR–camera alignment across heterogeneous and unsynchronized data sources. The method establishes robust 3D–2D correspondence through a dual-line strategy that anchors the calibration on structural priors—using device-specific main lines and surrounding auxiliary lines—to ensure stability under large viewpoint offsets. Unlike conventional autonomous-driving setups, the proposed framework addresses the geometric misalignment challenges inherent to cross-platform sensing, such as uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)–ground robot cooperation or multirobot inspection fleets, where rigid coupling is infeasible. Experiments on real-world power-system inspection data further validate the method’s accuracy and robustness, achieving subdegree alignment precision and enabling fine-grained disconnector state verification as a representative industrial use case. The results demonstrate that the proposed asynchronous calibration paradigm offers a general and practical solution for multisource fusion in complex industrial environments.

title: Joint Information-Theoretic Threshold Optimization and Error Bounds for MAP Decoding in Parallel Stochastic Quantizer Architectures
authors: Hassan Tavakoli, Thinh Nguyen, Bella Bose
abstract: This paper studies a Parallel Stochastic Quantizer architecture, in which multiple low-resolution comparators process an input signal in parallel, each corrupted by additive noise. We model the system as a discrete memoryless channel and analyze the mutual information (MI) between the input and the aggregated quantized output. To optimize information transfer, we propose an iterative algorithm that alternates between maximizing MI via momentum-accelerated gradient ascent and minimizing an upper bound on the MAP error probability using gradient descent. Theoretical analysis establishes that the MAP error probability decays exponentially with the number of parallel comparators, and the MI approaches the input entropy in the large-branch regime. Simulation results demonstrate the convergence of the algorithm, the dynamic adaptation of thresholds and noise parameters, and the tightness of the Bhattacharyya bound in predicting the system’s error probability. While minimizing the MAP error probability targets task-specific performance, maximizing mutual information provides a general-purpose objective that preserves task-relevant structure, ensuring robust performance across a wide range of downstream inference tasks. This joint optimization enables the system to achieve both low error rates and broad utility without being tailored to a single application. Our approach provides an efficient framework for designing low-power, high-speed quantization systems suitable for edge computing and sensing applications.

title: Integrated High-Order Mode Filtering using Cascaded Asymmetric Directional Couplers
authors: Mengyuan Ye, Guanyu Chen, Duowei Zeng, Siyuan Zhou, Tao Zhu
abstract: We propose and experimentally demonstrate a silicon-integrated high-order mode-pass filter based on cascaded asymmetric directional couplers (ADCs). The proposed design enables flexible selection of arbitrary higher-order modes while effectively filtering out all lower-order modes. As a proof of concept, we fabricated a second-order (TE₂) mode-pass filter. Experimental results show that the device achieves an insertion loss of less than 4 dB across the wavelength range of 1520–1580 nm, with extinction ratios for both TE₀ and TE₁ modes exceeding 15 dB. This compact and complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS)-compatible architecture offers a scalable solution for mode-selective filtering in silicon photonic MDM systems.

title: FuseRes: Robust In-Bed Respiration Monitoring System via Multi-modal Fusion of Millimeter Wave Radar and Wi-Fi Signals
authors: Chengjian Xing, Xiaolu Zeng, Xiaopeng Yang, Yu Liu, Huimin Hao, Miao Liu
abstract: Non-contact vital sign sensing has attracted increasing attention from both academia and industry. Millimeter-wave radar-based respiration sensing provides high accuracy but suffers from a limited field of view and strong dependence on target position and orientation, which restricts its practical deployment. In contrast, Wi-Fi-based sensing provides wide coverage and great resilience over device setup, yet its respiration estimation accuracy under ideal conditions is generally inferior to that of millimeter-wave radar. Consequently, the robustness of single-modal approaches in practical scenarios remains limited due to the inherent drawbacks of each system. This paper presents a fusion-based respiration monitoring system that integrates millimeter-wave radar and Wi-Fi signals. By jointly exploiting the high precision of millimeter-wave radar and the wide-area sensing capability of Wi-Fi, robust respiration monitoring is achieved in practical bedroom environments. To effectively utilize heterogeneous signals, a fusion decision scheme is designed to adaptively determine the necessity of signal fusion. Furthermore, a multi-modal signal fusion method based on multivariate signal processing is proposed to jointly extract the common respiration components shared across different modalities. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system, FuseRes, can robustly and accurately estimate respiration in complex real-world scenarios, supporting stable non-contact vital sign monitoring and facilitating practical deployment.

title: Classifier-Agnostic Ambiguity Detection Framework for Targeted Oversampling in Imbalanced Learning
authors: Takorn Prexawanprasut, Worapat Paireekreng
abstract: This paper introduces a classifier-agnostic framework for ambiguity detection in imbalanced datasets, designed to improve classification performance by isolating regions of high local uncertainty. Unlike traditional resampling methods that treat the entire dataset uniformly, our framework identifies ambiguous regions based on misclassified minority instances and quantifies local uncertainty using entropy estimated over k-nearest neighborhoods. A distribution-driven threshold selection mechanism dynamically selects high-entropy zones that indicate structurally complex and failure-prone areas in the feature space. These regions are further aggregated using clustering techniques to ensure a coherent spatial representation. The resulting ambiguous zones can guide targeted oversampling strategies that enhance model performance while avoiding overfitting in well-separated regions. The framework is modular and classifier-independent, enabling systematic comparison across different models. Extensive experiments on 34 benchmark datasets demonstrate that ambiguity-guided interventions significantly improve recall, F1-score, and G-mean compared to global SMOTE approaches. The proposed method offers a statistically grounded, scalable, and interpretable approach to localized resampling, making it suitable for deployment in high-stakes domains such as medical diagnosis and anomaly detection.

title: SSFNet: Spatial-Spectral Frequency-Aware Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Guanliang Wan, Danqing Liu, Yunxin Liu, Tengyue Yang, Yanhui Guo
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Spatial–spectral modeling methods have achieved remarkable progress in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification. However, most existing approaches primarily emphasize spatial–spectral feature fusion or multi-scale representation learning, while the intrinsic frequency-discriminative characteristics of spatial and spectral signals remain underexplored for coordinated local–global modeling. To address these issues, we propose a novel Spatial–Spectral Frequency-Aware Network (SSFNet) for hyperspectral image classification. SSFNet adopts a dual-branch architecture, consisting of a Frequency-Aware Spatial Modeling (FASM) branch and a Frequency-Based Spectral Modeling (FBSM) branch. The spatial branch (FASM) enhances boundary sensitivity and global contextual consistency through explicit high–low frequency modulation, enabling improved spatial representation learning. The FBSM branch performs spectral frequency decomposition, where the low-frequency component captures smooth, large-scale spectral trends, and the high-frequency component models fine-grained variations and discriminative fluctuations. A gated fusion mechanism is further designed to adaptively integrate complementary frequency features across different scales. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that SSFNet consistently outperforms eight state-of-the-art methods in terms of classification accuracy and generalization capability. These results verify the effectiveness of explicit frequency-aware spatial–spectral modeling for hyperspectral image classification.

title: CFO-Robust Detection for 5G PRACH under Fading Channels: Analytical Modeling and Performance Evaluation
authors: Daniel Alarcón-Martín, Mari Carmen Aguayo-Torres, Francisco J. Martín-Vega, Gerardo Gómez
abstract: The Physical Random Access Channel (PRACH) is essential for initial access and synchronization in both 5G and future 6G networks; however, its detection is highly sensitive to impairments such as high user density, large carrier frequency offset (CFO), and fast fading. Although prior studies have examined PRACH detection, they are often restricted to specific scenarios or lack a comprehensive analytical characterization of performance. We introduce a unified analytical framework that characterizes the statistical distribution of the received power delay profile (PDP) under flat Rayleigh fading and supports both coherent combining (CC) and power combining (PC) repetition strategies. For each strategy, we derive optimal threshold expressions and closed-form detection probabilities. Furthermore, we analyze two key cases depending on the coherence time: identical and independent channel realizations per repetition. Secondly, we exploit the correlation induced by CFO across cyclic shifts to design a novel low-complexity detector that exploits PDP dependencies. Numerical results indicate that PC outperforms CC when repetitions experience independent channels, while CC can be preferable under identical realizations in limited settings. On the other hand, the proposed CFO-aware detector delivers improved robustness under severe CFO conditions.

title: Lithium-Ion Battery SOH Estimation via Mixture Correntropy Loss-Based PCA and Kolmogorov-Arnold-Enhanced Liquid Neural Networks
authors: Wentao Ma, Zhuo Li, Peng Guo, Yang Li, Badong Chen
abstract: Accurate state of health (SOH) estimation of lithium-ion batteries is essential for reliable battery management but is particularly challenging under mixed non-Gaussian measurement noise and data scarcity. This article proposes a robust SOH estimation framework that combines noise-resistant feature extraction with data-efficient, interpretable continuous-time dynamic modeling. A mixture correntropy loss-based principal component analysis (MCL-PCA) method is first developed, which employs a hybrid Gaussian–Laplacian correntropy criterion to adaptively suppress mixed non-Gaussian noise and extract stable low-dimensional health features (HFs). These features serve as inputs to a newly designed Kolmogorov-Arnold-enhanced liquid neural network (KLNN), which augments continuous-time liquid dynamics with structured nonlinear mappings to improve stability and nonlinear generalization under limited small-sample conditions. These two components are integrated into a unified framework that yields coherent and physically consistent SOH degradation trajectories. Experimental results on laboratory and public datasets demonstrate that MCL-PCA significantly improves feature robustness, while KLNN achieves superior SOH estimation accuracy in noisy and small-sample scenarios, resulting in notably lower prediction errors than conventional PCA-based methods and consistent advantages over baseline models.

title: Four Quadrants Operations of Single-Stage CSI With Discharge Path in Motor Drives Applications
authors: Giovanni Migliazza, Emilio Carfagna, Fabio Immovilli, Emilio Lorenzani
abstract: Classical single-stage current source inverters (CSIs), i.e., without a prestage converter, powered by a DC voltage source can only operate when the output line-to-line voltages are greater than the input DC voltage, thanks to their inherent boost capability. The recently proposed single-stage CSI with discharge path is able to control the drive from zero speed to the maximum speed only in the first quadrant without the insertion of any additional prestage converter to control the input DC current. Thanks to a deeper analysis on the topology and on the control strategy, this work shows a new control scheme and, consequently, a space vector modulation able to cover the four quadrant operations of the machine without any use of bidirectional or additional switches or other modifications of the topology. The single-stage CSI with discharge path state-space equations are presented with the average model and verified through simulations. Stability analysis is considered through Nyquist criterion. Simulations and experimental results confirm the validity of the proposed solution for single-stage CSI with discharge path topology, paving the way for a broader adoption of this power converter in motor drive applications.

title: Enhancing Physical Layer Security in Modern Transmission Systems via Semantic Similarity–Aware Signal Processing
authors: Haythem Hayouni
abstract: Semantic similarity is explored as a novel dimension for enhancing physical layer security in wireless transmission systems. A new semantic-aware security model is proposed, in which transmitted signals are adaptively shaped according to their semantic alignment with the legitimate receiver. By embedding semantic similarity into the signal generation process, the proposed approach inherently limits information leakage to unintended receivers without relying on artificial noise, secret keys, or additional power. Analytical insights and numerical results demonstrate improved secrecy performance compared with conventional physical layer security schemes.

title: Integrated Multimodal Sensing, Communication, and Computation With Adaptive Splitting and Dynamic Routing in Satellite-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Jin Yang, Danpu Liu, Zhilong Zhang, Xinxin He
abstract: In real-time remote sensing scenarios, satellites typically acquire environmental data and relay it to ground data centers for inference, which incurs substantial latency and backhaul consumption. On-satellite inference has been explored to alleviate this overhead. However, the limited computation and energy budgets of a single satellite lead to processing latency on large-scale workloads. Accordingly, a collective inference of satellites and ground servers is utilized to realize compute-while-forwarding inference in an integrated multimodal sensing, communication, and computation (ISCC) framework. A central challenge is that sensing modality selection, deep neural network (DNN) splitting, and dynamic routing are tightly coupled in this scenario. Optimizing these dimensions separately is suboptimal under resource constraints. To manage the coupled multi-domain resources, a joint optimization problem is formulated to maximize the overall task inference accuracy while minimizing end-to-end latency and energy consumption. This problem jointly optimizes sensing modality selection, DNN layer splitting, dynamic routing, and resource allocation. To address this non-convex problem, a hybrid proximal policy optimization (HPPO)-based reinforcement learning algorithm is developed with a dual-actor structure handling discrete and continuous actions. The features related to network topology, transmission capacity, computational loads, and task requirements are extracted for a phase-aware sequential decision design aligning sensing, routing, and computation adaptive decisions with constraint-integrated reward shaping that stabilizes learning and preserves feasibility under time-varying network connectivity. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme can achieve 23.79% <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula> 51.04% improvements in system utility compared to baseline methods, demonstrating its effectiveness for resource management in satellite-terrestrial ISCC systems.

title: Coverage Optimization Strategy for UAV Ad Hoc Network Based on Multi-Objective Improved Cooperative Search Algorithm
authors: Qiaqia Yin, Li Yang, Liu He
abstract: To address the issues of insufficient coverage quality and high secondary deployment energy consumption in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) ad hoc network during the coverage process in indoor obstacle environments, this paper proposes a coverage optimization strategy based on a multi-objective improved cooperative search algorithm (MOICSA). The proposed strategy constructs a multi-objective optimization model that integrates the coverage quality index and secondary deployment energy consumption. Building upon this, three key improvements are introduced to the classical cooperative search algorithm (CSA): (1) the incorporation of fast non-dominated sorting to mitigate local optima trapping, (2) the integration of a crowding distance mechanism to enhance solution set diversity, and (3) the embedding of a dynamic Lévy flight strategy to balance global exploration and local exploitation capabilities. Simulation experiments demonstrate that, under equivalent computational cost, the multi-objective deep Q-network algorithm (MODQN) exhibits the lowest secondary deployment energy consumption, while its coverage quality is far lower than that of other comparative algorithms, eventually leading to its suboptimal comprehensive performance in multi-objective optimization. In contrast, the proposed MOICSA outperforms multi-objective particle swarm optimization (MOPSO), non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II), multi-objective virtual force-guided whale optimization algorithm (MOVFWOA), and multi-objective cooperative search algorithm (MOCSA), achieving improvements of 10.719%, 6.667%, 4.348%, and 7.368% in coverage quality index, respectively, while simultaneously reducing secondary deployment energy consumption by 17.697%, 15.784%, 28.206%, and 12.667%. These results confirm that the proposed strategy provides a reliable solution for deploying UAV swarms in obstacle environments.

title: NOMA-Enabled Covert and Fair Data Collection for Multi-UAV Wireless Network Under Imperfect CSI
authors: Menglong Cheng, Juan Li, Chaoxiong Ye, Byungjin Cho, Zheng Chang
abstract: This paper studies NOMA-enabled covert and fair short-packet data collection in multi-UAV networks under imperfect channel state information (CSI), assisted by a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) to serve Internet of Things (IoT) nodes (INs). Motivated by sensitive IoT missions, we consider a scenario in which suppressing detectability can be more restrictive than maximizing throughput, and where finite-blocklength transmission and link heterogeneity necessitate a joint treatment of covertness, reliability, and fairness. Accordingly, this paper first characterizes the feasible boundary of covert communication under imperfect CSI and constructs an optimization-compatible covert constraint formulation. Then, a joint optimization framework is proposed to maximize the minimum amount of data collected from all INs, by jointly optimizing INs’ transmit power, RIS phase shifts, and the 3D trajectories of UAVs. To address the strong coupling and high non-convexity in the short-packet reliable rate expression with respect to the power variables under imperfect CSI, a two-layer nested alternating optimization algorithm for 3D covert and fair data collection (TLNA-3DCF) is developed. In the inner layer, robust NOMA power allocation is performed under imperfect CSI; in the outer layer, the RIS phase shifts and UAV 3D trajectories are alternately optimized based on the inner-layer results, with feasibility enforcement and convex approximations used to ensure convergence and manageable complexity. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme, while strictly satisfying covert communication constraints, significantly improves the service level of edge INs, thereby enhancing fairness in data collection.

title: A Probabilistic REM Framework With Dynamic Genetic Beam Selection for Robust Indoor mmWave Communication
authors: Takumi Bushi, Kenji Ohira, Hideyuki Shimonishi, Toshiro Nakahira, Shoko Shinohara, Yusuke Asai
abstract: In mmWave-based cyber-physical systems (CPS), reliable connectivity for robot control requires real-time beamforming. We propose a hybrid radio environment map (REM) estimation framework that decomposes the radio environment into a deterministic linear component and a stochastic Gaussian process regression (GPR) component. This approach enables real-time optimization of beamforming patterns for multiple mobile robots, even when the number of robots exceeds the available beam resources. The accuracy of REM estimation is validated using measurement data obtained from an indoor mmWave private 5G system, while the performance of dynamic beam selection is evaluated through simulations to enable controlled and repeatable analysis. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed REM-guided beam selection reduces the probability of communication outages and improves signal reliability compared to the baseline beam selection strategies. These results indicate that probabilistic REMs can serve as an effective decision-support tool for beam control in indoor mmWave cyber–physical systems.

title: WildfireKR: An LLM-Agent Decision Support Framework for Autonomous Wildfire Spread Simulation in South Korea
authors: Eungyu Park, Jangwon Park, Taeyu Kim
abstract: Wildfire management in South Korea requires rapid scenario exploration tools that enable responders to evaluate potential fire spread under varying weather and fuel conditions within operationally relevant timeframes. This paper presents WildfireKR, an open-source decision support system (DSS) that leverages large language model (LLM) agent orchestration to automate end-to-end wildfire spread simulation. The system chains ten functional modules, seven domain-specific and three supporting, via structured LLM tool-calling, enabling users to initiate multi-scenario analyses via natural language or structured YAML configurations. Korea-specific calibration comprising approximately 4,700 Monte Carlo simulations across four historical fires established a broadleaf spread probability of 0.70, reducing mean area error by 47% relative to the Mediterranean-calibrated baseline. Evaluation on three historical wildfires (Samcheok 2022, Goseong 2019, Uljin 2022) yielded area ratios of 0.65–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.58\times $ </tex-math></inline-formula> relative to observed burned areas. The single underestimation (Goseong, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.65\times $ </tex-math></inline-formula>) is attributed to extreme wind-driven spotting beyond the simplified spotting model’s capability, while the overestimations are consistent with the expected behavior of suppression-free simulations. Supplementary spatial assessment using Sentinel-2 differenced Normalized Burn Ratio (dNBR) derived burn scars with perimeter-based reference delineation (morphological closing) produced Recall values of 0.89–0.99 and IoU of approximately 0.24, indicating that the simulated fire envelope captured the majority of the actual burned area, a property relevant for decision support where false negatives carry disproportionate consequences. For comparison, pixel-based reference delineation yields lower IoU (0.13–0.16), demonstrating that much of the apparent spatial disagreement arises from interior unburned enclaves within fire boundaries rather than fundamental model failure. Systems evaluation confirmed high repeatability (burned-area CV = 3.3–7.1%), operational efficiency (2.7 min vs. 2–4 h manual workflow), and robustness (8/8 adversarial input tests detected). The framework will be released under the MIT license upon acceptance and comprises approximately 1,600 logical lines of Python.

title: Beyond Single-Framework Architectures: A Systematic Evaluation and Hybrid Design for Scalable Multi-Agent Coordination
authors: Muhammad Saleem Khan, Muhammad Janas Khan, Muhammad Sharif, Sadaf Yasmin
abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent frameworks have emerged as powerful tools for complex coordination tasks, yet systematic comparisons across realistic, large-scale scenarios remain limited. This paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of three prominent frameworks, i.e., CrewAI, AutoGen, and LangChain, alongside a novel hybrid architecture that strategically combines their complementary strengths. Using CREW-WILDFIRE, a realistic wildfire response benchmark featuring heterogeneous agents and partial observability, we evaluated the framework’s performance across 17 task levels and 7 behavioral competencies. Our results reveal distinct architectural trade-offs: CrewAI achieves 34% higher task designation scores through hierarchical coordination but lacks adaptability; AutoGen demonstrates 28% superior plan adaptation via conversational feedback, yet incurs high token costs; LangChain offers maximum flexibility at the expense of a 42% increase in computational overhead. Based on these insights, we propose and validate a hybrid LangGraph-CrewAI architecture that achieves a 96.1% success rate while reducing token consumption by 76.2% and decision latency by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$14.5\times $ </tex-math></inline-formula> compared to pure CrewAI implementations. Our comprehensive validation across 51 episodes (17 scenarios <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times 3$ </tex-math></inline-formula> seeds) demonstrates perfect scores in task designation and agent capitalization competencies, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. This work provides evidence-based guidance for framework selection, highlights fundamental scalability challenges in current systems, and demonstrates that intelligent hybrid designs can overcome individual framework limitations while maintaining cost-efficiency.

title: Timely Trajectory Reconstruction in Finite Buffer Remote Tracking Systems
authors: Sunjung Kang, Vishrant Tripathi, Christopher G. Brinton
abstract: Remote tracking systems play a critical role in applications such as IoT, monitoring, surveillance and healthcare. In such systems, maintaining both real-time state awareness (for online decision making) and accurate reconstruction of historical trajectories (for offline post-processing) are essential. While the Age of Information (AoI) metric has been extensively studied as a measure of freshness, it does not capture the accuracy with which past trajectories can be reconstructed. In this work, we investigate reconstruction error as a complementary metric to AoI, addressing the trade-off between timely updates and historical accuracy. Specifically, we we consider a remote tracking system where the source evolves as a Wiener process and examine three policies, each prioritizing different aspects of information management: Keep-Old, Keep-Fresh, and our proposed Inter-arrival-Aware dropping policy. We compare these policies in terms of impact on both AoI and reconstruction error in a remote tracking system with a finite buffer. Through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations of queueing behavior, we demonstrate that while the Keep-Fresh policy minimizes AoI, it does not necessarily minimize reconstruction error. In contrast, our proposed Inter-arrival-Aware dropping policy dynamically adjusts packet retention decisions based on generation times, achieving a balance between AoI and reconstruction error. Our results provide key insights into the design of efficient buffer management policies for resource-constrained IoT networks.

title: Design of Polar Subcodes for Permutation Decoding
authors: Mikhail Chernikov, Peter Trifonov
abstract: novel construction of randomized polar subcodes and subcodes of extended BCH codes is presented. The proposed approach stems from a new heuristic for building polar code frozen set which minimizes the error probability of successive cancellation algorithm applied to permuted noisy codewords. The frame error rate (FER) of the proposed codes under permutationbased decoding is comparable to FER of existing polar subcodes under successive cancellation list or sequential algorithm.

title: RadioVAR: Fast Sampling-Free Radio Map Construction via Coarse-to-Fine Token Prediction
authors: Han Zhang, Yu Han, Hanhui Chen, Mengchen Yao, Yun Lin
abstract: Accurate spatial priors of received power spectral density (PSD) are important for communication-aware urban sensing and learning-based resource management. Sampling-free radio map (RM) construction avoids costly in-area measurements by directly constructing a full PSD-based radio map from environmental priors. However, mainstream diffusion-based methods incur high inference latency and lack an explicit coarse-to-fine generation path. To address these issues, we propose RadioVAR, a visual autoregressive (VAR) progressive framework for sampling-free RM construction. RadioVAR replaces iterative denoising with a feed-forward next-scale token prediction process, which substantially reduces inference computation and latency. RadioVAR first represents radio maps in a discrete multi-scale latent space using a frozen tokenizer, and performs coarse-to-fine next-scale token prediction conditioned on multi-channel priors (e.g., building layout, Tx location, and vehicle occupancy maps). We further introduce dynamic residual-token supervision to construct state-dependent token targets from the residual-to-go, improving training–inference alignment. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that RadioVAR improves reconstruction quality over diffusion-based baselines while significantly reducing inference latency, thereby offering a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off for edge deployments.

title: Symptom-Aware Adaptation of CLIP for Few-Shot Medical Image Classification_supp2-3685174.pdf
authors: Wenxu Wang
abstract: Vision-language pre-training model yields powerful general-purpose representations, yet its direct application to medical images is hindered by two key gaps: (i) low sensitivity to fine-grained pathological cues and (ii) semantic misalignment with structured clinical terminology. We propose SITSA, a framework that adapts a frozen CLIP backbone for data-scarce medical tasks without full fine-tuning. SITSA introduces three synergistic components: (1) MedVisExtractor, a depthwise-separable convolutional adapter that recalibrates patch tokens to emphasize subtle lesions; (2) SymSemLoRA, which injects low-rank updates into the text encoder conditioned on large-language-model-generated prompts that encode texture, location, and shape attributes; and (3) Prototype-Guided Alignment, a cross-modal module that binds image patches and prompt tokens via shared learnable prototypes. Extensive experiments on COVID-QU-Ex, OCTMNIST, and DERMAMNIST demonstrate that SITSA surpasses thirteen state-of-the-art baselines in both few-shot and base-to-novel generalization, achieving the highest harmonic mean accuracy while keeping the backbone frozen. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/enfantsRichesDeprimes/sitsa</uri>.

title: CNIE: Content-Aware Non-Transferable Information Extraction for Fine-Grained Visual Categorization
authors: Qiaoqi Li, Shuo Ye, Yunfeng Diao, Dan Guo, Yantao Wei, Shutao Xia, Zitong Yu
abstract: Non-transferable learning (NTL) aims to restrict a model's generalization ability to a target domain. Existing methods primarily focus on coarse-grained tasks. However, the problem of NTL at the fine-grained level remains largely unexplored, mainly due to the difficulty in distinguishing subtle inter-class variations within the source domain while simultaneously suppressing transferable patterns that may generalize to the target domain. To this end, this paper proposes CNIE for this challenge, and the framework consists of three key components. The Fine-grained Prompt Representation (FPR) module effectively activates the rich semantic knowledge embedded in the pre-trained model by introducing mixed-granularity prompts and transfers it to downstream tasks. The Content-Aware Decoupling Model (CDM) disentangles visual features into two orthogonal components: content and context. The context captures latent domain-specific information to guide domain-level suppression and enhance non-transferability. The Non-Transferable Information Extraction (NIE) module encodes subclass-specific content, which is constrained by an information bottleneck to learn a minimal sufficient representation that preserves the intrinsic causal semantics of fine-grained categories. Both qualitative results and quantitative visualizations on three FGVC-related NTL datasets consistently demonstrate that the learned fine-grained feature representations achieve optimal non-transferability to the target domain with minimal degradation in the source domain, while also establishing new state-of-the-art results in non-transferable fine-grained representation learning.

title: LoD-GS: Robust and Lightweight Gaussian Splatting SLAM for Real-Time Volumetric Scene Reconstruction
authors: Jiachen Wang, Seung-Hyun Kong
abstract: Real-time 3D reconstruction is becoming a key enabler for robotics, mixed reality, and autonomous vehicles. Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled high-fidelity volumetric modeling, and their integration with SLAM shows strong potential for real-time deployment. However, the substantial size of 3DGS models limits deployment on heterogeneous devices, while their rendering quality remains highly sensitive to tracking accuracy under motion blur and abrupt texture variations. In this work, we propose LoD-GS, a lightweight and robust 3DGS-SLAM framework that produces compact yet high-fidelity Gaussian scene representations for flexible deployment. LoD-GS integrates entropy-driven scene-complete volumetric mapping to improve pose quality and Gaussian initialization, a geometry-aware rendering quality optimizer that emphasizes near-field and structure-rich regions under limited optimization budgets, and a deployment-aware level-of-detail 3DGS compression module that enables adaptive resource-quality tradeoffs. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world office sequences demonstrate its effectiveness, reducing model size by up to 53.8%, increasing rendering FPS by up to 43.72%, and improving PSNR by up to 2.471 dB.

title: Non-Intrusive Contactless Gesture Recognition for Human-Robot Interaction
authors: Dimitrios Tsiakmakis, Massimiliano Iosi, Gastone Ciuti
abstract: As robots increasingly integrate into collaborative and assistive environments, natural gesture interpretation becomes crucial for effective human-robot interaction. While vision-based gesture recognition systems face privacy concerns and performance limitations under occlusion and varying lighting conditions, wearable sensor approaches often compromise user comfort and interaction naturalness. These challenges necessitate alternative approaches that balance recognition accuracy with user acceptance in real-world deployment scenarios. This work proposes a lightweight transformer-based feature extractor where test-time augmentation with majority voting is employed during inference to enhance classification performance. With approximately 2M trainable parameters, the network maintains reasonable efficiency on modern acceleration hardware. Its modular architecture also indicates an easy adaptation to a variety of sensor configurations. It reached an overall accuracy of 97.21%, with similarly high scores for f1-score, precision, and recall. The pipeline's inference duration of 16 ms on GPU and 40 ms on CPU demonstrate its suitability for real-time applications in collaborative robotics.

title: Natural Language Interfaces for Spatial and Temporal Databases: A Comprehensive Overview of Methods, Taxonomy, and Future Directions
authors: Samya Acharja, Kanchan Chowdhury
abstract: The task of building a natural language interface to a database, known as NLIDB, has recently gained significant attention from both the database and Natural Language Processing (NLP) communities. There have been many research works that study the problem of automatically converting natural language questions into database queries. With the proliferation of geospatial datasets driven by the rapid emergence of location-aware sensors, geospatial databases play a vital role in supporting geospatial applications. However, querying geospatial and temporal databases differs substantially from querying traditional relational databases due to the presence of geospatial topological operators and temporal operators. To bridge the gap between geospatial query languages and non-expert users, the geospatial research community has increasingly focused on developing NLIDBs for geospatial databases. Yet, existing research remains fragmented across systems, datasets, and methodological choices, making it difficult to clearly understand the landscape of existing methods, their strengths and weaknesses, available benchmarks, and opportunities for future research. Existing surveys on NLIDBs focus on general-purpose database systems and do not treat geospatial and temporal databases as primary focus for analysis. To address this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive survey of studies on NLIDBs for geospatial and temporal databases. Specifically, we first provide a detailed overview of datasets and evaluation metrics used to train and evaluate geospatial and temporal NLIDBs. We then study the taxonomy of the methods for geospatial and temporal NLIDBs and provide a comprehensive summary and comparative analysis of the existing methods. Our survey reveals recurring trends in existing methods, substantial variation in datasets and evaluation practices, and several open challenges that continue to hinder progress in this area. Based on these findings, we identify promising directions for future research to advance natural language interfaces to geospatial and temporal databases.

title: A U-Net based LSGAN Framework for 12-Lead ECG Synthesis in Wearable IoMT Systems
authors: Min Gu Kim, Jaehyo Jung
abstract: Wearable devices, such as smartwatches, offer a convenient way to monitor cardiac conditions through single- or few-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) recordings. However, these devices fail to provide the comprehensive diagnostic information available from the standard 12-lead ECG used in clinical settings. To address this technological gap, this paper proposes a novel framework for generating a complete 12-lead ECG from only two leads (Lead I and II). We designed a deep learning model based on a Least Squares Generative Adversarial Network (LSGAN) that employs a U-Net architecture as its generator. The U-Net effectively extracts multi-scale features and morphological details of the ECG signal, while the LSGAN framework ensures a stable training process and generates high-fidelity waveforms. Furthermore, an L1 loss was incorporated to enforce pixel-wise accuracy in the generated signals. Experimental results on the large-scale PTB-XL dataset, using a patient-wise split for evaluation, show that our proposed model achieves a high average Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) of 0.905 across all ten generated leads, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. These findings confirm that our model can reliably generate clinically significant 12-lead ECGs, presenting a practical solution for enhancing remote cardiac monitoring in the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) environment.

title: A Global-Local Data Clustering Framework With Adaptive Feature and Sample Weighting and Balanced Constraint
authors: Yong Peng, Mimi Jin, Yiyan Wang, Yuxin Chen, Feiwei Qin, Wanzeng Kong, Andrzej Cichocki
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Clustering remains a hot research topic in machine learning and data mining communities. The underlying data similarity serves as the foundation of clustering data into respective groups. However, since different features have different discriminative capabilities and different samples might correspond to different importance levels, it is inaccurate to capture the data similarity if we ignore any of them in real applications. By taking both into consideration, we propose an adaptive feature and sample weighting (AFSW) scheme to quantitatively measure the importance in the least square regression sense, serving as a global clustering. As a local complementary, a graph is utilized to facilitate cluster indicator matrix learning, leading to a global-local (GL) clustering framework. As a secondary contribution, an entropy-based balanced constraint is introduced to enforce the cluster sizes to be close, which is a reasonable assumption if we are clueless to a certain given data set. In concrete model formulation, two strategies respectively based on self-paced learning and exponential probability coefficient are designed to determine the sample importance. The formulated model, termed GLAFSW, is optimized efficiently within an alternating direction framework. Experiments are performed on both synthetic and benchmark data sets from diverse aspects. By comparing the proposed framework against the state-of-the-arts, the results verified that it achieved competitive performance in clustering.

title: Target Speaker Isolation for Mobile Health Assessment from Speech with Crosstalk
authors: Apiwat Ditthapron, Adam C. Lammert, Emmanuel O. Agu
abstract: Speech assessments are crucial for monitoring medical conditions such as Parkinson’s disease, depression, and Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI). However, crosstalk—speech from non-target speakers—presents a critical challenge in real-world mobile health assessments, often leading to false diagnoses. Continuous, real-world speech assessments using mobile devices are realistic but require additional pre-processing steps to ensure speech quality. Isolating the target speaker’s speech is necessary before health assessments. Previous research has focused on removing background noise but paid limited attention to crosstalk in passive health assessments. Some prior work addressed crosstalk using speech separation, which is found to create artifacts on the extracted speech. Instead, we propose Target Speaker Isolation with Normal Distribution (TSI-N) to isolate speech samples of the target speaker. Unlike standard embeddings, TSI-N utilizes an N-vector speaker representation trained to fit an unbounded normal distribution for each speaker cluster. This probabilistic property enables robust null-hypothesis testing and rapid online learning on resource-constrained mobile devices. In rigorous evaluations on various levels of crosstalk, TSI-N significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving accuracy improvements of up to 36% for TBI detection and 18% for depression detection in passive speech assessments.

title: Multirate Finite-Control-Set Motor Parameter-Free Predictive Control for SPMSM Drives
authors: Xuesong Wu, Bowei Li, Cheng Xue, Yunwei Li
abstract: Compared to conventional single-rate schemes, the multirate finite-control-set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) allows higher switching frequencies at relatively low sampling rates. However, multirate schemes exhibit increased sensitivity to parameter variations, particularly as the number of subcycles increases. Although motor parameter-free prediction techniques can partially mitigate this sensitivity, their effectiveness within a multirate framework remains constrained by control parameters and nonlinear factors. To address these challenges, this work proposes an improved multirate FCS motor parameter-free predictive control (FCS-MPFPC) scheme. First, an adaptive control-parameter-tuning approach based on Kalman filters is introduced, which utilizes current variation information to recursively estimate the impact of input voltage. Second, corrected voltage vectors accounting for dead-time effects are incorporated into both the prediction process and observers. Furthermore, rotor movement effects are compensated using second-order generalized integrators (SOGI) and Lagrange interpolation. Experimental results validate the proposed method, demonstrating improved performance under both nominal and mismatched parameter conditions.

title: Forgettable Federated Linear Learning With Certified Data Unlearning
authors: Ruinan Jin, Minghui Chen, Qiong Zhang, Xiaoxiao Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The advent of federated learning (FL) has revolutionized the way distributed systems handle collaborative model training while preserving user privacy. Recently, federated unlearning (FU) has emerged to address demands for the “right to be forgotten” and unlearning of the impact of poisoned clients without requiring retraining in FL. Most FU algorithms require the cooperation of retained or target clients (clients to be unlearned), introducing additional communication overhead and potential security risks. In addition, some FU methods need to store historical models to execute the unlearning process. These challenges hinder the efficiency and memory constraints of the current FU methods. Moreover, due to the complexity of nonlinear models and their training strategies, most existing FU methods for deep neural networks (DNNs) lack theoretical certification. In this work, we introduce a novel FL training and unlearning strategy in DNN, termed forgettable federated linear learning (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> considers a common practice of using pretrained models to approximate DNN linearly, allowing them to achieve similar performance as the original networks via federated linear training (<monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FLT</monospace>). We then present <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FedRemoval</monospace>, a certified, efficient, and secure unlearning strategy that enables the server to unlearn a target client without requiring client communication or adding additional storage. We have conducted extensive empirical validation on small- to large-scale datasets, using both convolutional neural networks and modern foundation models (FMs). These experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> in balancing model accuracy with the successful unlearning of target clients. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathtt {F^{2}L^{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> represents a promising pipeline for efficient and trustworthy FU. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://anonymous.4open.science/r/2F2L-Federated-Unlearning-D57D/README.md</uri>

title: Scalable neural pushbroom architectures for real-time denoising of hyperspectral images onboard satellites
authors: Ziyao Yi, Davide Piccinini, Diego Valsesia, Tiziano Bianchi, Enrico Magli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The next generation of Earth observation satellites will seek to deploy intelligent models directly onboard the payload in order to minimize the latency incurred by the transmission and processing chain of the ground segment, for time-critical applications. Designing neural architectures for onboard execution, particularly for satellite-based hyperspectral imagers, poses novel challenges due to the unique constraints of this environment and imaging system that are largely unexplored by the traditional computer vision literature. In this paper, we show that this setting requires addressing three competing objectives, namely high-quality inference with low complexity, dynamic power scalability and fault tolerance. We focus on the problem of hyperspectral image denoising, which is a critical task to enable effective downstream inference, and highlights the constraints of the onboard processing scenario. We propose a neural network design that addresses the three aforementioned objectives with several novel contributions. In particular, we propose a mixture of denoisers that can be resilient to radiation-induced faults as well as allowing for time-varying power scaling. Moreover, each denoiser employs an innovative architecture where an image is processed line-by-line in a causal way, with a memory of past lines, in order to match the acquisition process of pushbroom hyperspectral sensors and greatly limit memory requirements. We show that the proposed architecture can run in real-time, i.e., process one line in the time it takes to acquire the next one, on low-power hardware and provide competitive denoising quality with respect to significantly more complex state-of-the-art models. We also show that the power scalability and fault tolerance objectives provide a design space with multiple tradeoffs between those properties and denoising quality.

title: Counterfactual Risk Minimization for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
authors: Yanhua Yang, Muli Yang, Jiahua Li, Henan Wang, Cheng Deng, Hongyuan Zhu
abstract: The out-of-distribution (OOD) property in data is deemed as one main challenge hindering the generalization ability of machine learning algorithms. However, the underlying reasons for this property remain an intriguing and open question that has yet to be fully understood. In this paper, we seek to enhance our understanding of the OOD phenomenon by framing it as a problem of distribution shift and addressing it through two complementary causal perspectives. The first is a generative causal view that elucidates the data generation process. We introduce a novel three-dimensional coordinate system to represent three fundamental distribution shifts, illustrating their role in various OOD generalization problems. The second is an anti-causal view that focuses on the model learning process. We develop an effective approach dubbed Counterfactual Risk Minimization (CRM) to address arbitrary distribution shifts in a unified framework. Additionally, we introduce a new multidomain visual recognition dataset called CONA to facilitate further exploration of OOD generalization. We conduct evaluations of CRM alongside several state-of-the-art competitors on four benchmark datasets under the three distribution shifts. The results not only affirm CRM’s superiority but also shed light on potential future directions.

title: Analog Implementation of a Fractional-Order PID Controller for the T200 Thruster Using a Novel Lattice Type Structure
authors: Yunus Emre Yokuş, Aslihan Kartci, Umut Engin Ayten, Roman Sotner, Jaroslav Koton
abstract: The T200 thruster is a widely used propulsion system for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs); however, achieving high-performance control with low energy consumption remains a challenge due to inherent system nonlinearities and uncertainties. Fractional-order PID (FOPID) controllers offer enhanced flexibility and robustness compared to classical PID structures, making them well-suited for such complex dynamic systems; however, their practical analog realization and algorithm-based parameter optimization remain limited in the existing literature. This paper presents a unified control–hardware co-design framework for the T200 thruster by combining metaheuristic optimization, FOPID control theory, and low-power analog circuit design. Within this framework, a transfer function of the T200 thruster is obtained from experimental input–output data, and the parameters of the FOPID controller are optimized using multiple metaheuristic algorithms to enhance dynamic performance. A novel lattice-type circuit structure is then introduced for the analog implementation of the optimized FOPID controller. Comparative performance evaluation using transient response criteria and a series of statistical analysis methods demonstrates that the Salp Swarm Algorithm provides the most consistent and effective tuning results, yielding a well-damped closed-loop response with zero overshoot, improved transient behavior, and strong robustness against disturbances and parameter variations. SPICE simulations of the proposed lattice-type analog implementation show close agreement with MATLAB-based control results while achieving low power consumption of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$22.902~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>W and reliable operation under process, voltage, temperature, and noise variations. These results confirm the feasibility and advantages of algorithm-optimized FOPID controllers for next-generation marine robotic systems in which high-precision control and low power consumption are crucial.

title: Pruned and Quantized Hybrid Models for Edge-Based Automatic Modulation Recognition
authors: Yerin Byeon, Dohyun Kim, Yue Cao, Wansu Lim
abstract: Learning-based automatic modulation recognition on edge devices is envisioned as a critical enabler of real-time spectrum management in future 6G Internet of Things networks. In addition to the requirement of accurate modulation detection, edge devices pose the challenge of reducing the size of the automatic modulation recognition model. This paper proposes a lightweight hybrid model of multi-channel convolutional neural network and the mobile vision transformer. The proposed model addresses both accuracy improvement and model size reduction by employing weight-based pruning and post-training dynamic range quantization. Performance results in edge computing environments such as the Raspberry Pi and Jetson platforms using the RadioML 2016.10a dataset show that the proposed model achieved up to a 91% reduction in memory usage compared to its original version prior to pruning and quantization and demonstrated up to an 8% improvement in average accuracy compared to the baseline convolutional neural network model.

title: Decoding kinematic parameters of the whole arm using spikes from area 2 of primary somatosensory cortex
authors: Parinaz Mohammadi, Matin Asgharpour, Mohammad Reza Daliri
abstract: The development of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) has primarily focused on decoding motor commands from the primary motor cortex (M1) to restore mobility in individuals with severe neuromuscular disabilities. As neuroprosthetic control technologies advance, integrating afferent feedback mechanisms to provide proprioceptive and tactile awareness has become increasingly important. The primary somatosensory cortex (S1), particularly Area 2, serves as a specialized hub for proprioception by integrating multimodal muscle and cutaneous inputs. Although previous studies have demonstrated that Area 2 encodes whole-arm kinematics more effectively than hand-only models, the potential for high-accuracy directional classification to serve as BCI command triggers has not been fully investigated. This study evaluated the efficacy of decoding whole-arm movement directions using spiking activity recorded from 100-electrode arrays implanted in Area 2 of two rhesus macaques (Monkeys C and H) during a center-out reaching task. The experimental paradigm comprised two conditions: active (voluntary) reaches and passive (involuntary) movements induced by a 2N external force. Mean Firing Rates were used as primary features, with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied for dimensionality reduction and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) employed to classify movement directions across four and eight principal axes. Our results demonstrate near-perfect classification accuracies, averaging 99.84% ± 0.53 for active trials and 94.28% ± 6.24 for passive trials across both subjects. Crucially, voluntary movements were decoded significantly more accurately than involuntary movements (p<0.001), supporting the hypothesis that S1 integrates efference-copy signals from motor areas to enhance the estimation of limb state. These findings validate the robust representation of whole-arm kinematics in S1 and establish Area 2 as a vital source of discriminative signals for future closed-loop, afferent neural interfaces.

title: Analyzing and Characterizing the Split-Transformer LLC Converters
authors: Jinheng Dong, Hongjun Wang, Cheng Cheng, Shaojun Xie, Haichun Liu, Wenming Chen
abstract: An LLC converter with non-equal-turns-ratios split-transformer (NET-ST) has been proposed to address the large rectified current pulsation in the conventional LLC converters. However, due to the complex resonant processes, the conventional fundamental harmonic approximation is not applicable for analyzing this converter and there is a lack of guidance on designing the NET-ST LLC converter. A data fitting analysis method based on simulation data is proposed to investigate the influence of the parameter KN (defined as the ratio of two transformers turns ratios, i.e., KN = n1/n2) on characterizing the NET-ST LLC converter. This method establishes the quantitative relationship between the parameter KN and both the current pulsation and total losses, and reveals the underlying design principles along with the optimal range for KN. The analysis and experimental results of a 1600W, 400V-input/400V-output prototype demonstrate that the NET-ST design reduces the current pulsation by approximately 40% and losses by about 10% compared to the conventional design. The proposed methodology for analyzing and designing the NET-ST LLC converters has also been promoted to design a split-transformer (ST) LLC named NEI-ST with equal-turns-ratios but non-equal magnetizing inductance. The demonstrated designs validate the effectiveness of presented method and show the appliable of the ST LLC converters.

title: Reparameterization-Driven Depthwise Separable Large-Kernel Network for Lightweight Salient Object Detection of Strip Steel Surface Defects
authors: Xiaofei Zhou, Zhenkun Mo, Gongyang Li, Liuxin Bao, Xiaobin Xu, Jiyong Zhang
abstract: With the rapid development of neural networks, strip steel surface defect detection, as an important task in computer vision, has achieved remarkable progress. However, state-of-the-art methods still face a tradeoff between accuracy and efficiency. High-performing models are usually large and computationally expensive, whereas lightweight models often suffer from limited detection accuracy. To address this issue, we first propose a spatial channel enhancement (SCE) module, which consists of a reparameterizable depthwise large-kernel convolution and a reparameterizable pointwise (RepPw) convolution. The proposed SCE module enlarges the receptive field and strengthens long-range spatial and channel interactions while preserving computational efficiency. Based on the SCE module, we propose a novel lightweight saliency model for strip steel surface defects, namely, reparameterization-driven depthwise separable large-kernel network (RepDSLKNet). RepDSLKNet employs SCE modules to build an encoder and a decoder, and utilizes cascaded channel attention (CCA) modules for the feature fusion. The lightweight architecture can effectively extract and fuse the semantic information and detailed features of strip steel surface defects, thereby improving the accuracy and speed of detection with a small model size. With an input size of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$224\times 224$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, our RepDSLKNet has only 0.47 M parameters and 0.42 G FLOPs during inference. Compared to the current state-of-the-art methods, our approach achieves a 19-fold improvement in throughput and a twofold reduction in latency. Experiments on two public strip steel defect datasets demonstrate that RepDSLKNet delivers competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods.

title: A Hybrid Heuristic Spectral Efficient Chunk Allocation, Relay Selection, and Deep Learning-Empowered Energy-Efficient Power Control in Multi-user OFDMA-PNC
authors: Ehsan Atefat Doost, Jonathan Rodriguez, Mário Antunes, Pablo Fondo-Ferreiro, Antonio Navarro
abstract: In this paper, we study joint spectral- and energy-efficient resource allocation in a multi-user, multi-relay orthogonal frequency-division multiple access physical-layer network coding (OFDMA-PNC) two-way relay network, where paired user equipments communicate via decode-and-forward relay nodes under global energy efficiency (GEE) maximization. The underlying problem couples chunk-level assignment, relay selection, and subcarrier-level power control into a non-convex mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) formulation with binary allocation variables and continuous power variables, which is computationally prohibitive to solve optimally at practical network scales. To address this challenge, we propose a hybrid framework that first performs a heuristic joint chunk allocation and relay selection based on a three-dimensional tensor of quality-of-service (QoS)-filtered active subcarriers and then applies a supervised deep neural network (DNN) to approximate the optimal power allocation generated by a fractional programming–based solver. The heuristic algorithm operates in a reduced two-dimensional search space with collision-avoidance rules that ensure feasibility with respect to chunk and relay constraints, while the DNN-based power control module is trained on labeled samples and uses a projection step to preserve all node-wise power and QoS constraints at inference time. Simulation results for a single-cell scenario with 8 user pairs and 8 relays show that the proposed joint heuristic scheme achieves spectral efficiency close to an mixed-integer linear programming (MILP)-based alternating-optimization (AO) benchmark and significantly higher than a purely greedy heuristic baseline, while the DNN-based power control attains near-optimal energy efficiency with an acceleration rate of about 15× compared to iterative optimization. Overall, the combined heuristic–DNN framework reduces computational complexity by approximately 9.94×10⁴ relative to an exact AO-based method and about 3× relative to the heuristic AO scheme, providing a scalable and real-time-capable solution for energy-efficient resource allocation in large-scale OFDMA-based decode-and-forward (DF)-PNC systems.

title: GOSPA and T-GOSPA quasi-metrics for evaluation of multi-object tracking algorithms
authors: Ángel F. Garcá-Fernández, Jinhao Gu, Lennart Svensson, Yuxuan Xia, Jan Krejčí, Oliver Kost, Ondřej Straka
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper introduces two quasi-metrics for performance assessment of multi-object tracking (MOT) algorithms. One quasi-metric is an extension of the generalised optimal subpattern assignment (GOSPA) metric and measures the discrepancy between sets of objects. The other quasi-metric is an extension of the trajectory GOSPA (T-GOSPA) metric and measures the discrepancy between sets of trajectories. Similar to the GOSPA-based metrics, these quasi-metrics include costs for localisation error for properly detected objects, the number of false objects and the number of missed objects. The T-GOSPA quasi-metric also includes a track switching cost. Differently from the GOSPA and T-GOSPA metrics, the proposed quasi-metrics have the flexibility of penalising missed and false objects with different costs, and the localisation costs are not required to be symmetric. We also explain how to obtain similarity score functions based on these quasi-metrics. The performance of several Bayesian MOT algorithms is assessed with the T-GOSPA quasi-metric via simulations.

title: LearningFlow: Automated Policy Learning Workflow for Urban Driving with Large Language Models
authors: Zengqi Peng, Yubin Wang, Xu Han, Lei Zheng, Jun Ma
abstract: Recent advancements in reinforcement learning (RL) demonstrate the significant potential in autonomous driving. Despite this promise, challenges such as the manual design of reward functions and low sample efficiency in complex environments continue to impede the development of safe and effective driving policies. To tackle these issues, we introduce LearningFlow, an innovative automated policy learning workflow tailored to urban driving. This framework leverages the collaboration of multiple large language model (LLM) agents throughout the RL training process. LearningFlow includes a curriculum sequence generation process and a reward generation process, which work in tandem to guide the RL policy by generating tailored training curricula and reward functions. Particularly, each process is supported by an analysis agent that evaluates training progress and provides critical insights to the generation agent. Through the collaborative efforts of these LLM agents, LearningFlow automates policy learning across a series of complex driving tasks, and it significantly reduces the reliance on manual reward function design while enhancing sample efficiency. Comprehensive experiments are conducted in the high-fidelity CARLA simulator, along with comparisons with other existing methods, to demonstrate the efficacy of our proposed approach. The results demonstrate that LearningFlow excels in generating rewards and curricula. It also achieves superior performance and robust generalization across various driving tasks, as well as commendable adaptation to different RL algorithms.

title: Optimizing Incoming Quality Control with a Dynamic Set Covering Strategy for Zero-Defect Inspection
authors: Wenqing Zhou, Yufeng Chen, Rui Zhou, Mohamed Sharaf
abstract: This paper develops an adaptive incoming quality control framework that integrates a dynamic set-covering strategy to support zero-defect inspection in large-scale production, ensuring complete identification of non-conforming parts while reducing inspection time and cost. Conventional set-covering approaches typically guarantee full coverage at limited batch sizes, and often fail to maintain both detection completeness and operational efficiency in large-scale production. To address this limitation, we reformulate the inspection process using a two-phase dynamic set-covering mechanism. In the first phase, a baseline set is established by hierarchically prioritizing inspection items. Items detecting more non-conforming parts are assigned a higher priority. The remaining items that do not contribute to current coverage are subsequently ranked according to their inherent defect probabilities. In the second phase, a reduced inspection item subset is selected from the prioritized set to cover all observed non-conforming parts in the current batch. The proposed strategy operates through two interconnected mechanisms to ensure practical defect coverage. It establishes explicit coverage constraints and employs an adaptive update rule that refines item priorities across batches based on residual inspection information. Finally, we present an example to demonstrate the proposed approach.

title: Modulation-Agnostic Interference Cancellation Using Approximate Message Passing Driven by Empirical Bayes
authors: Haochuan Zhang, Zhiyi Liang, Huimin Zhu
abstract: Interference cancellation (IC) is crucial for sustaining high-performance massive MIMO downlink receivers at cell edges, where capacity is severely limited by strong co-channel interference. Achieving Bayes-optimal recovery of this interference is fundamentally challenging, as it typically requires explicit prior knowledge of the interfering modulation formats. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel, modulation-agnostic interference estimation approach that unfolds the Approximate Message Passing (AMP) framework, replacing its standard static denoiser with a dynamic Empirical Bayes (EB) component. In this synergistic architecture, AMP systematically decouples the spatial MIMO channel to provide the scalar environment required by EB, while EB adaptively learns the prior distribution and infers the signal without any pre-existing modulation knowledge. This approach completely circumvents the need for brute-force exhaustive searches, rendering it significantly more efficient than conventional model selection methods, while exhibiting superior computational scalability compared to Expectation-Maximization (EM) based AMP. We integrate this EB-AMP estimator into a comprehensive joint detection framework that iteratively alternates among data detection, signal subtraction, interference recovery, and interference cancellation. Numerical simulations demonstrate that under the assumptions of perfect channel state information (CSI), i.i.d. Rayleigh fading, and a single interfering cell, the proposed IC receiver effectively suppresses unknown interference at power levels comparable to the desired data signal, achieving an estimation accuracy that closely approaches the theoretical interference-free bound.

title: Compressed Sensing for Efficient Near-Field Scanning of Embedded Systems
authors: Vishnuvardhan V. Iyer, Jacob D. Rezac, James C. Booth
abstract: We propose a novel compressed sensing (CS) approach for high-resolution near-field electromagnetic (EM) cartography for embedded system blocks. Assuming near-fields can be represented using a truncated plane-wave expansion, the CS approach estimates optimal solutions of coefficients in this expansion using considerably fewer samples than the theoretically defined Nyquist rate. The method is demonstrated in two phases: First, the simulation phase enables us to build and demonstrate the CS framework on a source model that mimics near fields from computing chips. Several simulation experiments are implemented to verify the compressibility of the dataset and the limits within which CS can be applied. Second, the measurement phase validates the methodology by implementing CS-based scans on a commercial test board and reconstructing the field maps expected from high-resolution scans. Experiments in the measurement phase also demonstrate the utility of the CS approach for anomaly detection using near-field measurements. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method by showing at least <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> and as much as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> reduction in sample requirements, in order to reach less than 10<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> relative error using the CS-based method compared to Nyquist-style sampling in simulations and measurements.

title: Impact of TSV Quantity Scaling on TDDB Reliability and Failure Mechanisms in TSV Interposers
authors: Fa Wu, Yahui Mao, Shuwen Zeng, Juan Tang, Sijie Tian, Kai Yuan, Lun Zhang, Huang Wenjun, Jun Shen, Zhaohuan Tang
abstract: This article investigates the impact of through-silicon via (TSV) quantity on time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB) lifetime and the associated failure mechanisms. Experimental results demonstrate a pronounced reduction in TDDB lifetime with increasing TSV quantity, and the corresponding quantity-dependent scaling factors are systematically extracted. TDDB failure locations are precisely localized using a combination of low-light InGaAs infrared (IR) microscopy and focused ion beam (FIB) analysis. Finite element analysis (FEAs) reveals severe electric field crowding at the TSV bottom corners, mainly induced by copper sharp-edge geometries, which dominate the dielectric breakdown behavior. These results elucidate the physical origin of quantity-dependent TDDB degradation and provide device-level design guidelines for improving the TDDB reliability of TSV interposers.

title: FairCLIP: Bridging Visual Diversity and Linguistic Representation for Equitable Image Synthesis
authors: Xinxing Li, Guangyong Zheng, Jiacheng Liu, Wenli Chen
abstract: Ensuring fairness in text-to-image generation remains a challenging problem. Existing solutions often rely on costly retraining, large attribute-balanced datasets, or natural-language prompt interventions that remain vulnerable to linguistic ambiguity. To address these limitations, we propose FairCLIP, a plug-and-play, visually guided prompt-learning framework that uses reference images to learn lightweight attribute-aware prompt embeddings, termed FairTokens, in a pretrained multimodal embedding space. FairTokens are optimized with three complementary objectives—direction alignment, semantic consistency, and a generation-time fairness regularizer—to provide visually grounded attribute control while preserving the semantics of the original prompt. Experiments on CelebA and FairFace indicate that FairCLIP improves attribute balance in many evaluated single- and multi-attribute settings relative to text-only baselines while maintaining competitive image quality under standard metrics such as FID. We further analyze residual failure cases and discuss the limitations of perceived-attribute benchmarks and the KL-divergence proxy used for distributional fairness assessment. Overall, FairCLIP provides a lightweight representation-level approach for mitigating certain distributional skews in generated samples, while highlighting the need for broader evaluation and deployment-aware fairness analysis.

title: Detecting Abnormal Vehicle Behavior with a Diffusion-Transformer Generation Framework
authors: Zhiyu Wang, Linheng Li, Xu Qu, Bin Ran
abstract: With the rapid development of intelligent transportation systems (ITS), ensuring traffic safety has become a critical societal priority. Abnormal driving behavior detection has emerged as an essential approach to mitigate potential traffic risks, as even minor abnormal behaviors can lead to severe safety hazards through cascading effects under specific environmental conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an innovative framework for the detection of abnormal vehicle behavior, built on the deep integration of a diffusion model and the Transformer architecture. The proposed framework adopts a two-stage process: a generation stage, where a classifier-free denoising diffusion model reconstructs the normal trajectory distribution from Gaussian noise iteratively, and a comparison stage, where deviations between observed trajectories and generated normal trajectory distributions are quantified to detect anomalies. This method eliminates the need for hand-made thresholds, overcoming the subjectivity and rigidity of traditional anomaly detection approaches, and effectively addresses the severe class imbalance between normal and abnormal samples often observed in real-world traffic scenarios. In addition, extensive evaluations of real-world traffic datasets demonstrate the superiority of the framework, achieving a precision of 0.948, a precision of 0.317, a recall of 0.765, and an F1-score of 0.448, significantly exceeding state-of-the-art benchmarks. These results underscore the potential of the proposed framework as a robust foundation for proactive early-warning systems in ITS, contributing to safer and more efficient transportation networks.

title: Contrastive Learning for Terrain Identification of Earthworm-Like Robots with Limited Labeled Proprioceptive Data
authors: Hongsen Pang, Hanzhuo Zhang, Yunfei Zheng, Hongbin Fang
abstract: Accurate terrain perception is essential for autonomous decision-making in earthworm-like robots, which navigate complex environments using retrograde peristaltic gaits. During locomotion, these robots generate proprioceptive feedback through ground interaction, making visionless terrain recognition possible. However, conventional learning-based methods typically rely on large labeled datasets, which are costly and impractical for field-deployed earthworm-like robots. In this study, we develop an inertial sensor system to capture the robot’s proprioceptive signals and propose a contrastive learning framework for efficient terrain identification with minimal supervision. The model employs data augmentation strategies—both strong and weak—to generate diverse views of the same data, facilitating robust feature extraction. A Temporal Convolutional Network and autoregressive Transformer are used to capture multi-scale temporal dependencies from raw IMU signals. To further enhance performance under limited labeled data, we introduce a semi-supervised fine-tuning stage, which generates pseudo-labels for unlabeled data and applies supervised contrastive loss to refine the learned representations. Extensive experiments across varied terrains and gaits show that our method consistently outperforms existing supervised and self-supervised baselines, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal labeled data. This approach addresses a key challenge in proprioceptive terrain recognition and offers a scalable, efficient solution for real-time deployment in resource-constrained earthworm-like robotic platforms.
